justification: All pass criteria met: JWT/API-key auth per consumer type correctly designed; internal traffic fully separated via mTLS mesh bypassing gateway; business logic explicitly excluded ('Don't embed business logic in the gateway'); SPOF risk identified as Critical with horizontal scaling mitigation; webhook delivery addressed with dedicated async dispatcher, queue, retry, dead-letter, HMAC signatures; flexibility_points and rigidity_points both present with concrete examples; technology recommendation (Kong/Envoy) with build-vs-buy consideration and swappability noted.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough internal traffic separation — mTLS mesh with network policy enforcement, not just a mention but a fully designed component with clear rationale
Webhook delivery architecture is production-grade: async queue, exponential backoff, dead-letter queue, HMAC signatures, per-partner circuit breakers, CloudEvents spec
Phased migration plan with dual-auth period avoids big-bang risk — shows operational maturity
Rigidity points are intentional and well-justified (network isolation as security invariant, header contract, webhook signature scheme)
Explicitly calls out what does NOT belong in the gateway (business logic, authorization, data persistence) in both the component table and pitfalls section
Rate limiting design is nuanced — sliding window, embedded to avoid extra hop, fail-open on Redis failure, separate keys per consumer type

Could have discussed managed gateway services (AWS API Gateway, GCP Apigee) more explicitly as a buy option alongside self-hosted Kong/Envoy — the build-vs-buy trade-off is present but leans toward build
Auth Service cache staleness risk with TTL, pub/sub invalidation, and revoked tokens list shows depth of thought on operational concerns

agent_output_excerpt:
## API Gateway Architecture — Logistics Platform

### Executive Summary

The platform needs a unified API gateway layer that consolidates authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation currently scattered across individual services. I recommend a **single gateway with three distinct ingress pipelines** (mobile, partner, internal) rather than three separate gateways, keeping operational complexity low while enforcing strict traffic isolation. This eliminates inconsistent auth implementation
